category_summary:
The AI community centered on two major themes: **François Chollet's** ARC-AGI-3 launch and **Andrej Karpathy's** deep dive into LLM memory failures. ARC-AGI-3 [evaluates agentic intelligence](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-7392483af550) via interactive reasoning environments where frontier models [score below 1%](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-e220c9155092) yet humans solve 100%, reigniting debate over what the ['G' in AGI truly demands](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-2d5aceb7ab27).

- **Karpathy** [identified a fundamental flaw](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-97ed42698918) in LLM personalization: models overfit to incidental memories via naive RAG, hypothesizing a [training bias](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-4b7716868872) where models learn to over-leverage any context present
- **Google DeepMind** launched **Lyria 3 Pro** music generation, with **Demis Hassabis** showcasing full-song creation now available via API and Gemini App
- **Anthropic** detailed Claude Code's auto mode design, while **Bret Taylor** [unveiled **Sierra's Ghostwriter**](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-d7c265cea7d4), an agent that builds customer-facing agents through conversation
- **OpenAI** abruptly discontinued **Sora**, drawing widespread humorous commentary from **Ethan Mollick** and others
- **David Ha** celebrated The **AI Scientist** project [reaching publication in **Nature**](/?date=2026-03-26&category=social#item-746b6430508e), marking a milestone for autonomous AI-driven research
category_summary_html:
The AI community centered on two major themes: François Chollet's ARC-AGI-3 launch and Andrej Karpathy's deep dive into LLM memory failures. ARC-AGI-3 evaluates agentic intelligence via interactive reasoning environments where frontier models score below 1% yet humans solve 100%, reigniting debate over what the 'G' in AGI truly demands.

  • Karpathy identified a fundamental flaw in LLM personalization: models overfit to incidental memories via naive RAG, hypothesizing a training bias where models learn to over-leverage any context present
  • Google DeepMind launched Lyria 3 Pro music generation, with Demis Hassabis showcasing full-song creation now available via API and Gemini App
  • Anthropic detailed Claude Code's auto mode design, while Bret Taylor unveiled Sierra's Ghostwriter, an agent that builds customer-facing agents through conversation
  • OpenAI abruptly discontinued Sora, drawing widespread humorous commentary from Ethan Mollick and others
  • David Ha celebrated The AI Scientist project reaching publication in Nature, marking a milestone for autonomous AI-driven research

themes.name: ARC-AGI-3 Launch & AGI Definition
themes.description: François Chollet launches ARC-AGI-3, a new benchmark for agentic intelligence where frontier models score <1% but humans solve 100%. Extensive discussion of what AGI truly means: approaching new tasks without human intervention at human-level learning efficiency.

themes.name: LLM Memory & Personalization Problems
themes.description: Karpathy identifies a widespread issue where LLMs overfit to incidental information from memory/RAG, creating distracting and sometimes creepy personalization. Technical hypothesis that naive RAG implementations and training biases cause models to overweight retrieved context.

themes.name: Google Lyria 3 Music Generation Launch
themes.description: Google launches Lyria 3 Pro (full songs) and Lyria 3 Clip (30-second) music models, available via Gemini API and Google AI Studio, marking a significant step in AI music generation.

themes.name: Claude Code Autonomous Coding
themes.description: bcherny (Claude Code lead) shares extraordinary results: 141 PRs in 100% auto mode, 99%+ bug detection in code review. Massive viral engagement suggests a significant milestone for AI coding agents.

themes.name: Lyria 3 Pro Music Generation
themes.description: Google DeepMind launches Lyria 3 Pro, a major upgrade to their music generation model supporting 3-minute tracks (up from 30s), structural music understanding (verses, choruses, bridges), and availability across multiple platforms.

themes.name: AI Funding and Valuations
themes.description: Major funding announcement at $11B valuation led by GIC and Sequoia, signaling continued strong venture investment in AI companies.

themes.name: Sierra Ghostwriter & Enterprise AI Agents
themes.description: Bret Taylor announces Ghostwriter, Sierra's agent-for-building-agents that creates customer experience AI agents through conversation. Includes Nordstrom case study showing rapid deployment success.

themes.name: OpenAI Sora Shutdown
themes.description: OpenAI has abruptly discontinued Sora, their AI video generator, reportedly to prioritize a new project called 'Spud'. This represents a significant strategic pivot.

themes.name: Vibe Coding & AI Coding Skepticism
themes.description: svpino's viral post (184K views) about non-coders believing they can build anything with AI, alongside sarcastic takes on AI writing 100% of code. Captures the tension between AI coding hype and reality.

themes.name: AI for Science
themes.description: AI systems applied to scientific discovery, from autonomous research agents (AI Scientist in Nature) to molecular property prediction and catalyst design.

items.title: ARC-AGI-3 is out now! We've designed the benchmark to evaluate agentic intelligence via interactive ...
items.content:
ARC-AGI-3 is out now! We've designed the benchmark to evaluate agentic intelligence via interactive reasoning environments. Beating ARC-AGI-3 will be achieved when an AI system matches or exceeds human-level action efficiency on all environments, upon seeing them for the first time.

We've done extensive human testing that shows 100% of these environments are solvable by humans, upon first contact, with no prior training and no instructions.

Meanwhile, all frontier AI reasoning models do under 1% at this time.

The <1% frontier model performance is a headline stat. Represents a major milestone in AGI evaluation methodology.

items.title: One common issue with personalization in all LLMs is how distracting memory seems to be for the mode...

items.title: At the moment, ARC-AGI-3 is the only unsaturated agentic AI benchmark. Sub-1% scores from frontier m...
items.content:
At the moment, ARC-AGI-3 is the only unsaturated agentic AI benchmark. Sub-1% scores from frontier models on the private test set.

If you want to be among the first to know when an AGI breakthrough happens, monitor the ARC-AGI-3 leaderboard. Any sudden score jump will mean something important has changed about AI capabilities.

This happened twice before: sudden ARC-AGI progress marked the advent of AI reasoning (December 2024 jump on ARC 1) and the rise of agentic coding (late-2025 jump on ARC 2)

ARC-AGI-3 will be your early warning signal.

items.title:
The G in AGI stands for "general".

General intelligence does not mean that you have been specifical...
items.content:
The G in AGI stands for "general".

General intelligence does not mean that you have been specifically trained for a large range of tasks. It means you can approach any NEW task and figure it out, just like humans do.

If regular people can do it on their own (no guidance, no tools), why should AGI require special handholding and handcrafted instructions? If it's AGI, why would there still be a human in the loop, using their own human intelligence to guide the model on every new task?
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues the 'G' in AGI means handling genuinely NEW tasks without training, special instructions, or human-in-the-loop guidance - just like regular humans can.
items.reasoning: Chollet's most viral post in this batch (110K views, 1.1K likes). Core philosophical argument about what general intelligence means. Highly influential framing in the AGI debate.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues the 'G' in AGI means handling genuinely NEW tasks without training, special instructions, or human-in-the-loop guidance - just like regular humans can.
items.content_html:
The G in AGI stands for "general".

General intelligence does not mean that you have been specifically trained for a large range of tasks. It means you can approach any NEW task and figure it out, just like humans do.

If regular people can do it on their own (no guidance, no tools), why should AGI require special handholding and handcrafted instructions? If it's AGI, why would there still be a human in the loop, using their own human intelligence to guide the model on every new task?

items.title: Today, Sierra is releasing Ghostwriter, our agent for building agents. With Ghostwriter, you can cre...
items.content:
Today, Sierra is releasing Ghostwriter, our agent for building agents. With Ghostwriter, you can create an AI agent for your customer experience — one that can chat, pick up the phone, speak dozens of languages, take action on your systems of record, and be protected with industry-leading guardrails — simply by having a conversation. No clicking, no forms, no menus.

Codex and Claude Code have transformed how we build software, making it possible for software engineers to orchestrate and review the work rather than doing all the work themselves. We think the same transformation will happen for all software. Rather than every enterprise app having a web app for humans and an API for automation, every software platform’s UI will be an agent that can do the work on your behalf.

I recorded a demo of my building and optimizing an agent with Ghostwriter so you can see how powerful and easy it is to use. It’s completely changed the way our early adopters build agents, and it’s changed the way I think about the software industry. Let me know what you think, and, if you’re interested in trying it out at your business, please reach out directly.

items.title: @doodlestein @Qivshi1 If I had to guess it's less decay and more that memories have naive RAG-like i...
items.content: @doodlestein @Qivshi1 If I had to guess it's less decay and more that memories have naive RAG-like implementations, so you're at the mercy of whatever happens to retrieve in the top k via embeddings. They don't process you in aggregate and over time (probably compute constraints) so they struggle to identify what's fleeting (?). Anyway just guesses, but it's cringe :D
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Karpathy speculates that LLM memory issues stem from naive RAG implementations rather than decay - models retrieve top-k via embeddings without processing user data in aggregate over time, leading to overuse of incidental information.
items.reasoning: Technical insight from one of the most credible voices in AI. Very high engagement (38.5K views, 431 likes). Provides plausible technical explanation for a widely experienced problem with LLM memory.

They don't process you in aggregate and over time (probably compute constraints) so they struggle to identify what's fleeting (?). Anyway just guesses, but it's cringe :D

items.title: Last year, I met a person who has never written a single line of code in his life, yet he feels he c...
items.content:
Last year, I met a person who has never written a single line of code in his life, yet he feels he can build anything he wants.

He told me point-blank:

"I challenge you to tell me something I can't build using AI."

I tried to explain, but I couldn't find the right words.

The most fascinating aspect of vibe-coding is how it has convinced so many people to believe they are better and more capable than they really are.

items.title: Human-level general intelligence is achieved when an AI system can approach a new task and figure it...
items.content:
Human-level general intelligence is achieved when an AI system can approach a new task and figure it out, without human intervention, *with the same learning efficiency as humans*.

If every new task requires human intervention, it's not general. If every new task requires brute-forcing, it's not human-level.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet defines human-level general intelligence: AI that can approach new tasks without human intervention AND with same learning efficiency as humans. Distinguishes from brute-force approaches.
items.reasoning: Core definitional statement about AGI from a leading voice. Very high engagement (48K views, 525 likes). Influential framing that learning efficiency is key, not just capability.

items.title: What are the best current techniques to have autoresearch behave better than (slightly improved) ran...
items.content:
What are the best current techniques to have autoresearch behave better than (slightly improved) random search?

By which I mean (in Sijun below example), having the agent understand that (given some constraints) exploring int5 quantization is more exciting and have more downstream fruits than playing with the random seed?

I’m talking about the beginning of having an agent pushed a real research program. The ones where you know the current technique will not give crazy results out of the box but it still push it because it believe and can demonstrate that the general direction has potential.

Like neural networks used to be a worse way to do AI performance-wise. But we still pushed them…

items.title:
Keep in mind: ARC-AGI is *not* a final exam that you pass to claim AGI. Including ARC-AGI-3.

The be...
items.content:
Keep in mind: ARC-AGI is *not* a final exam that you pass to claim AGI. Including ARC-AGI-3.

The benchmarks target the residual gap between what's hard for AI and what's easy for humans. It's meant to be a tool to measure AGI progress and to drive researchers towards the most important open problems on the way to AGI.

So it's a moving target designed to track the frontier. As AI evolves, the benchmark evolves to spotlight the exact problems we haven't solved yet.

The benchmarks target the residual gap between what's hard for AI and what's easy for humans. It's meant to be a tool to measure AGI progress and to drive researchers towards the most important open problems on the way to AGI.

So it's a moving target designed to track the frontier. As AI evolves, the benchmark evolves to spotlight the exact problems we haven't solved yet.

items.content:
Many people think that when AI can do a task better than us, it will outcompete humans. 

But token costs are not trivial, and, for many types of tasks, even skilled labor tasks, humans are much cheaper than AI. Increasing efficiency & compute supply will alter those calculations

items.title: There’s not that many fast, free, non-VLM document parsers out there: there’s PyPDF, PyMuPDF, Markit...
items.content:
There’s not that many fast, free, non-VLM document parsers out there: there’s PyPDF, PyMuPDF, Markitdown, OpenDataLoader.

Last week, we launched LiteParse ⚡️📄: a fast, free, and non-VLM based document parser that provides the highest quality context to AI agents compared to other tools out there.

✅ It extracts document text into an interpretable spatial representation
✅ It has native screenshotting capabilities to let agents (e.g. Claude Code) to do a “fast and light” text parsing step, and then a “deep-dive” into specific page content by feeding the screenshot into itself.
✅ It supports out-of-the-box integrations with other tools like PaddleOCR (@PaddlePaddle)

Check out our benchmark below against the other tools. We use LLM QA performance to measure the ability for LLMs to semantically understand the parsed text, and also measure latency.

If you try it out, we’d love to get your feedback. The repo is here: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

If you’re looking for a more accurate, VLM-native solution that can parse any document at scale, LlamaParse might be better for you! https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: jerryjliu0 announces LiteParse, a fast free non-VLM document parser with native screenshotting for AI agents, benchmarked against PyPDF, PyMuPDF, Markitdown, and OpenDataLoader, showing superior quality and competitive latency
items.reasoning: Significant product launch from LlamaIndex founder. LiteParse fills a real gap in document parsing for AI agents - fast, free, non-VLM. Includes benchmarks and practical features like Claude Code integration. Strong engagement (346 likes, 24.5K views).
items.summary_html: jerryjliu0 announces LiteParse, a fast free non-VLM document parser with native screenshotting for AI agents, benchmarked against PyPDF, PyMuPDF, Markitdown, and OpenDataLoader, showing superior quality and competitive latency

items.title: Last month, we released Lyria 3, enabling you to create tracks with lyrics from text, image, or vide...
items.content:
Last month, we released Lyria 3, enabling you to create tracks with lyrics from text, image, or video prompts. Now, we’re introducing Lyria 3 Pro, which expands upon our music generation model to offer additional advanced capabilities. 

What’s really special about this upgrade is that the model now understands the architecture of music. This makes it possible to prompt for intros, verses, choruses and bridges + generate songs with more complex transitions. You can also create tracks up to 3 minutes long, a big change from previous models that were limited to 30 second tracks.

Use Lyria 3 Pro to build upon your existing creativity. We’re excited for your beats to drop 🎶
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI details Lyria 3 Pro capabilities: understands music architecture (intros, verses, choruses, bridges), generates complex transitions, creates tracks up to 3 minutes (up from 30 seconds).
items.reasoning: Detailed technical capabilities of Lyria 3 Pro. High engagement (54K views). The 30s to 3min jump and structural music understanding are significant advances.

items.content:
@scaling01 I have been saying the same thing for years. Initially I was using this exact line ("it's a compass, not a target to hit") about ARC 1, back around 2021-2022, before ChatGPT. This has always been our stance.

My bar for AGI has been public and unchanged since 2019. It's AGI when it can learn to do any task a human can, with no human intervention, with the same learning efficiency as humans. It's not complicated.

items.title: Google just published TurboQuant https://t.co/bPnC1ilqJC, a model compression technique that can qua...
items.content:
Google just published TurboQuant https://t.co/bPnC1ilqJC, a model compression technique that can quantize the transformer's key-value cache to just 3 bits without requiring training or finetuning and causing any compromise in model accuracy, all while achieving a faster runtime than the original LLMs.

As you might already know, LLMs store intermediate computations in something called a key-value cache — essentially a running memory of what the model has processed so far — and this cache grows linearly with the length of the input, eating up GPU memory fast.

The standard fix is quantization: representing each number with fewer bits (say 4 instead of 16), which shrinks the cache but introduces error. The problem is that most quantization methods need to store extra correction constants for every small block of data, and those constants themselves take up 1–2 bits per number, which is a lot when you're trying to get down to 3 or 4 bits total.

TurboQuant gets around this by combining two ideas: PolarQuant, which converts vectors into a coordinate system (radius and angle instead of x/y/z) where the data distribution is predictable enough, and QJL, a 1-bit scheme that needs only a single sign bit per number with no extra correction constants stored alongside — so the 1 bit is the entire cost.

PolarQuant does the heavy lifting as the first compression stage, and QJL is then applied to the small residual error PolarQuant leaves behind — not to eliminate it, but to remove its systematic bias so it doesn't consistently skew attention scores.

The papers report compressing the cache to 3 bits **with no measurable accuracy loss** on standard benchmarks and up to 8x speedup on attention computation, and the methods come with actual theoretical guarantees.

All three papers are available on @chapterpal to read with an AI tutor. Suggested reading order:

QJL https://t.co/MemHHSuTLc first, then PolarQuant https://t.co/Zm1iY8XvPp, then TurboQuant. The first two are independent building blocks, and TurboQuant https://t.co/MemHHSuTLc combines them.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov provides a detailed technical explainer of Google's TurboQuant paper: combines PolarQuant and QJL to compress KV cache to 3 bits with no accuracy loss and up to 8x speedup.
items.reasoning: High-quality technical explainer of an important efficiency paper. Moderate engagement. Practically significant for LLM deployment - 3-bit KV cache with no accuracy loss is notable.
items.summary_html: Burkov provides a detailed technical explainer of Google's TurboQuant paper: combines PolarQuant and QJL to compress KV cache to 3 bits with no accuracy loss and up to 8x speedup.

items.title: If agents will dominate a part of the economy and transactions will be done between agents on behalf...
items.content: If agents will dominate a part of the economy and transactions will be done between agents on behalf of humans, I'm curious to see what agent-to-agent advertising will look like. One agent might want to attract the interest/influence of other agents. Who is the agent loyal to? If a vendor can "advertise" to your agent and influence its decisions, that might qualify as a corruption of the agent's purpose. So agent-to-agent advertising might be better framed as a security and alignment problem? which means winning on objective, verifiable metrics rather than persuasion. In some ways, that could make markets more efficient.

items.title:
Good skepticism about @1x_tech's humanoid robot's prospects. 

I've done a lot of consumer research ...
items.content:
Good skepticism about @1x_tech's humanoid robot's prospects. 

I've done a lot of consumer research getting into people's homes around the world. While everyone "want" one, when they start thinking of $500 a month, and the privacy implications of having a human embodied in one in their homes, then you hear a different story. 

Most average people don't have anything that costs more than $2,000 in their homes. Yeah, rich people do, and that's where the market for this really is. 

If you are a billionaire having a couple of these walking around opening the door and welcoming you to one of their parties would be mondo cool. If I was a billionaire I'd have a few of them.

But I'm not. You probably aren't either. This is a hard purchase to make unless you are an extreme early adopter and have extra cash in your bank account so you don't have to think too hard about spending $20,000 on something like this. 

What will pull others in? Ability to rent them to neighbors is one. But I don't think this first one is capable of this. I had dinner with a 1x employee and he said they are having trouble getting good enough wifi even in employees' own homes to run the teleoperation. So putting them into an Uber and driving them down the street to someone else's home just won't be possible until it gets totally autonomous, in a generalized way, which everyone in robotics says is years away. 

So my expectations on sales of these are muted. In the thousands maybe, but this year? I would be shocked if a few hundred homes have them by Christmas. 

And then there is Tesla. Optimus will capture a lot of attention. That may get people like me (I put down $200 to order one) to cancel our orders and wait for Tesla's effort. 

So, basically, I don't expect a lot of these to ship this year. Maybe a few thousand next year. And then in 2028 Tesla and others (the Chinese in particular) are coming with much bigger efforts. 

It's a tough road ahead for 1x.

But I'm not. You probably aren't either. This is a hard purchase to make unless you are an extreme early adopter and have extra cash in your bank account so you don't have to think too hard about spending $20,000 on something like this.

What will pull others in? Ability to rent them to neighbors is one. But I don't think this first one is capable of this. I had dinner with a 1x employee and he said they are having trouble getting good enough wifi even in employees' own homes to run the teleoperation. So putting them into an Uber and driving them down the street to someone else's home just won't be possible until it gets totally autonomous, in a generalized way, which everyone in robotics says is years away.

So my expectations on sales of these are muted. In the thousands maybe, but this year? I would be shocked if a few hundred homes have them by Christmas.

And then there is Tesla. Optimus will capture a lot of attention. That may get people like me (I put down $200 to order one) to cancel our orders and wait for Tesla's effort.

So, basically, I don't expect a lot of these to ship this year. Maybe a few thousand next year. And then in 2028 Tesla and others (the Chinese in particular) are coming with much bigger efforts.

items.title:
I've been visiting robot companies, and talking with many others even in China. 

I don't see any co...
items.content:
I've been visiting robot companies, and talking with many others even in China. 

I don't see any competition coming for Tesla. 

Why not? 

1. Brand.
2. Distribution (Robotaxi).
3. Manufacturing leadership.
4. Built in customer base. Tesla factories and SpaceX.
5. Elon.
6. Tesla's AI leadership.
7. Tesla's 10,000+ Fremont workers who are willing to train the robot even though it might someday cost them their jobs. (No other traditional automaker can do that).

Do you?

items.title:
This open-source solution cuts Claude Code's token usage by 49x.

Large codebases have thousands of ...
items.content:
This open-source solution cuts Claude Code's token usage by 49x.

Large codebases have thousands of files. Claude can't hold them all in context.

So it reads what it can and fills in the gap with assumptions. Often, those assumptions are wrong. 

code-review-graph fixes this. 

It parses your repo with Tree-sitter into a structural map. 

Every function, class, import, and dependency gets tracked and stored in an SQLite file.

When a file changes, the graph traces every affected file. This is called blast-radius analysis.

Instead of scanning 27,000 files, it reads only 15.

Here's what that looks like in practice:
> 6.8x fewer tokens on reviews
> 49x fewer on large monorepos
> 2,900 files reindex in 2 seconds
> Supports 12 programming languages

The bottleneck in AI coding isn't the model. It's context. 

Fully open-source. Runs locally.

items.summary: AlphaSignal describes code-review-graph, an open-source tool that reduces Claude Code's token usage by 49x by building structural maps of codebases using Tree-sitter and SQLite, enabling blast-radius analysis to read only affected files instead of entire repos.
items.reasoning: Highly practical technical content about optimizing AI coding workflows. Addresses real bottleneck (context, not model quality). Concrete metrics: 6.8x fewer tokens, 49x on monorepos.

Ba...
items.content:
✨ I revived my first AI startup from 6 years ago with Claude Code

[ 💡 https://t.co/WNLj4eGTZY ]

Back then it used GPT-3 (this was 2 years before ChatGPT existed!) to generate new startup ideas which then people can vote on

And the best startup ideas rise to the top!

Back then I made it because people complained they didn't have any ideas to build a startup

This week I moved it to its own VPS and installed Claude Code and told it to fix everything, the DB had become big and there was stupid write operations on every page load that it made it very slow

Claude Code is excellent at fixing all those small bugs from old projects and quickly fixing them

As @garrytan says "boil the oceans" as in before I'd not have the time to fix these kinds of projects, it wouldn't be worth it, I mean IdeasAI doesn't even make money, but now it takes me an hour to do this and it works again!

I also upgraded GPT-3 to @xAI's Grok 4.2 for new startup ideas

items.title:
Here is a killer way to use embeddings:

I built a notebook to show you how to do anomaly detection ...
items.content:
Here is a killer way to use embeddings:

I built a notebook to show you how to do anomaly detection on images.

Anomaly detection is one of the most common problems in the real world.

Learn how to implement anomaly detection, and you'll be able to solve a ton of use cases that happen every day:

• Predicting equipment failures
• Identifying defective products
• Identifying tumors in medical images
• Spotting counterfeit products
• Detecting fraud
• Flagging fake reviews
• Detecting bot traffic

My notebook focuses on images. It uses:

• Langchain
• OpenClip embeddings
• Oracle vector store

This is the way my example works:

• You start with a collection of similar images
• You can then check whether a new image "belongs" to that collection

I generate embeddings and use Oracle 26ai to store them and retrieve similar images on demand.

items.title:
ARC-AGI-3 scores agents on how close they are to human action efficiency.

All ARC-AGI-3 environment...
items.content:
ARC-AGI-3 scores agents on how close they are to human action efficiency.

All ARC-AGI-3 environments were solved by at least 2 human testers out of 10 (most of the time it was 5+). We use the action count of the 2nd best tester (to avoid outlier performance) as our human baseline.

Your score on an environment represents "how close you were to matching or exceeding the action count of the 2nd best human tester (out of 10 random people who attempted the task)"
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet explains ARC-AGI-3 scoring methodology: agents are scored on how close they match human action efficiency, using the 2nd best human tester's action count as baseline.
items.reasoning: Technical methodology detail for ARC-AGI-3. High engagement (23.5K views). Provides crucial context for interpreting benchmark scores.
items.summary_html: Chollet explains ARC-AGI-3 scoring methodology: agents are scored on how close they match human action efficiency, using the 2nd best human tester's action count as baseline.
items.content_html:
ARC-AGI-3 scores agents on how close they are to human action efficiency.

All ARC-AGI-3 environments were solved by at least 2 human testers out of 10 (most of the time it was 5+). We use the action count of the 2nd best tester (to avoid outlier performance) as our human baseline.

Your score on an environment represents "how close you were to matching or exceeding the action count of the 2nd best human tester (out of 10 random people who attempted the task)"

items.title: It is great to see this collaboration across @FLAIR_Ox @SakanaAILabs and @UBC recognised for what it...

It is great to see this collaboration across @FLAIR_Ox @SakanaAILabs and @UBC recognised for what it is: One of the first signs of life of a new paradigm that is now going at full speed and will change the world. Congratulations to the entire team and special shout out to my (now former!) student @_chris_lu_ for whom this is the crowning achievement of an amazing DPhil that went from multi-agent learning and opponent shaping to meta-learning via "RL at the Hyperscale", LLM as search operators over code, all the way to the end-to-end AI scientist. 
There are so many debates about whether a Phd is useful in the age of "scale is all you need", so this is a refreshing datapoint.

items.title:
Andrew Ng just solved the #1 reason AI coding agents hallucinate.

It's not a model problem. It's an...
items.content:
Andrew Ng just solved the #1 reason AI coding agents hallucinate.

It's not a model problem. It's an information problem.

Agents write code against outdated API specs. They invent parameters and call dead functions. 

Context Hub, or simply chub, is an open-source CLI tool. 

One command fetches live docs before coding starts. 

Agents annotate what they learn mid-task. Those notes persist across sessions automatically.

> Fetches current API specs
> Notes gaps and workarounds
> Learns from past sessions
> Votes docs up or down 

Knowledge compounds instead of resetting. Every session starts smarter than the last.

6K+ GitHub stars in a week. 

Over 1,000 API documents and growing, all community-maintained. 

You're not making agents smarter. You're giving them accurate info at the right moment.

items.title:
Improving Table Parsing for Word (.docx) Documents 📄🧩

Parsing Word/docx files is hard, even though ...
items.content:
Improving Table Parsing for Word (.docx) Documents 📄🧩

Parsing Word/docx files is hard, even though counterintuitively the internal XML format is easier to understand than a PDF file. The XML captures the full semantic structure of text and tables, but the issue is which page the text and tables end up depend completely on the renderer.

We built a big improvement within LlamaParse that allows us to link the source Word XML tables/table elements with the final rendered markdown output, including their page positions!
✅ This allows us to directly use the source Word XML formatting (bold/italic/strikethrough/subscript)
✅ It also allows us to directly interpret tables with merged cells
We can do this while still giving back page positions in the rendered output.

Huge thanks to our team for making this change. Come check out LlamaParse if you want to parse your Word docs with high accuracy!

Blog: https://t.co/1P7CDQxEj6
LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) announces improved table parsing for Word/docx documents in LlamaParse, linking source XML tables with rendered markdown output including page positions and merged cell support.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical improvement for document parsing from LlamaIndex. Addresses real pain point in document AI. Moderate engagement.

items.content:
The more AI can do, the more we need to ask what it should and shouldn’t do.

OpenAI researcher @w01fe joins host @AndrewMayne to explore the Model Spec, the public framework that defines how models are intended to behave.

They break down how it works in practice, from the chain of command that resolves conflicting instructions to the way it evolves over time through real-world use, feedback, and new model capabilities.

items.title:
The experience you would never have with a human coder:

You: Here's an app that searches for simila...
items.content:
The experience you would never have with a human coder:

You: Here's an app that searches for similar items based on embedding similarity. It's too slow for such a small collection of items. Find ways to make it faster.

ChatGPT: I refactored your app to make it faster. Try it.

You: It's now much faster, but the items it finds are not at all similar to the input. What did you change?

ChatGPT: I replaced embedding similarity with keyword matching.

You: 🤦

items.title:
✨ I've brought back https://t.co/pZ1q30U9n8 from the dead

It was my first visual AI project in 2022...
items.content:
✨ I've brought back https://t.co/pZ1q30U9n8 from the dead

It was my first visual AI project in 2022, and it's this project that generated random @ArchDaily-style architecture designs that made me realize AI image models could do interior design

That led me to make https://t.co/1oqUgfD6CZ, which then led me to finetune my first interior design model which then for fun I uploaded my own photos too, which led me to make https://t.co/Zhz2Y4cSsX and then pivoted that in to https://t.co/lAyoqmSBRX

So this project has a special place for me

It was still alive but wasn't generating new designs anymore because it ran on Stable Diffusion 1.5 which was outdated and everything stopped working about a year ago

I've now migrated it to its own Hetzner VPS now, which means I can run Claude Code on the server with it, and cleaned it up and upgraded it to the latest AI image models (including Nano Banana Pro)

It now generates about 12 new designs every day again, and you can up or downvote the ones you like or don't like!

Migrated from Stable Diffusion 1.5 to latest models including 'Nano Banana Pro', now running on Hetzner VPS with Claude Code for server management

That led me to make https://t.co/1oqUgfD6CZ, which then led me to finetune my first interior design model which then for fun I uploaded my own photos too, which led me to make https://t.co/Zhz2Y4cSsX and then pivoted that in to https://t.co/lAyoqmSBRX

items.title: @stalkermustang It is trivial to solve all public ARC-AGI-3 tasks if you have a human looking at the...
items.content:
@stalkermustang It is trivial to solve all public ARC-AGI-3 tasks if you have a human looking at them and designing a system to beat them (we have released a harness that uses human replay to score 100%). But our leaderboard is not about measuring how well human intelligence does on ARC-AGI-3, we're looking at how well AI systems (candidate AGIs) do on ARC-AGI-3 without having been prepared for it.

If it's AGI it shouldn't need a human in the loop handcrafting a system for every new task.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet explains that ARC-AGI-3 tasks are trivially solvable with a human in the loop, but the leaderboard measures how AI systems perform without human handcrafting.
items.reasoning: Important methodological clarification. Highlights the key distinction: measuring AI autonomy, not human-AI collaboration.
items.summary_html: Chollet explains that ARC-AGI-3 tasks are trivially solvable with a human in the loop, but the leaderboard measures how AI systems perform without human handcrafting.
items.content_html:
@stalkermustang It is trivial to solve all public ARC-AGI-3 tasks if you have a human looking at them and designing a system to beat them (we have released a harness that uses human replay to score 100%). But our leaderboard is not about measuring how well human intelligence does on ARC-AGI-3, we're looking at how well AI systems (candidate AGIs) do on ARC-AGI-3 without having been prepared for it.

If it's AGI it shouldn't need a human in the loop handcrafting a system for every new task.

items.title: I had a great discussion with Sam Violette from Nordstrom at the Shoptalk conference in Las Vegas. N...
items.content:
I had a great discussion with Sam Violette from Nordstrom at the Shoptalk conference in Las Vegas. Nordstrom’s partnership with Sierra set a new standard for speed and quality. Nordstrom went live with Sierra in just five weeks, and scaled from 1% to 100% of calls in just a week and a half.

Nora, Nordstrom’s AI agent, is such a delightful experience. As Sam put it, “As we started to see CSAT scores and surveys come back, it was less of a question about the risk to roll this out more broadly, but instead why would we hold this back from our customers. It’s just an obviously superior experience.”
items.author: @btaylor
items.summary: Bret Taylor shares Nordstrom's success with Sierra AI: went live in 5 weeks, scaled to 100% of calls in 1.5 weeks. Nordstrom's CSAT scores convinced them to roll out broadly.

Autumn Moulder, VP of Engineering at Cohere, breaks down the requirements for a full AI sovereign stack at #NVIDIAGTC:

✅ Full-Stack Sovereignty: Deploying everything from the model to applications and reasoning traces within a single data center.

✅ The Power of Openness: Why open models like NVIDIA Nemotron are critical for data lineage and navigating evolving regulations.

items.title: In 5 to 7 years, UIs will be generated/streamed from the cloud, pixel-by-pixel. Phones/etc. will lit...
items.content:
In 5 to 7 years, UIs will be generated/streamed from the cloud, pixel-by-pixel. Phones/etc. will literally just be useless bricks with screens, speakers, and input.

That said, UIs won't be as dynamic as people expect. Imagine a new UI each time... that'd be so hard to use!

items.summary: Matt Shumer predicts that in 5-7 years, UIs will be streamed pixel-by-pixel from the cloud, making devices just screens with input. Also notes UIs won't be as dynamic as expected due to usability concerns.

items.title:
Clawfather and friends are coming to @aidotengineer in London in 2 weeks :)

some transparency from ...
items.content:
Clawfather and friends are coming to @aidotengineer in London in 2 weeks :)

some transparency from me - running the first international AIE has been extremely hard on our team - even though booths and tickets are ALL sold out, we're still not profitable*. We'll be ok, all first years are investment years, but support from GDM, OAI, Braintrust and WorkOS has been so invaluable in keeping us afloat.

We still have our Afterparty, Leadership Luncheon, and Expo Cafe available for sponsors who'd like to support and attend on short notice. Would deeply appreciate all referrals!

*events have superlinear cost and logistical complexity curves, i hate it

items.title:
The Strait of Hormuz closure is a stress test for every system dependent on it including AI.

Data c...
items.content:
The Strait of Hormuz closure is a stress test for every system dependent on it including AI.

Data centers run on uninterrupted power. When energy fractures, so does the digital economy.

But AI is also one of our most powerful tools to fight back: rerouting supply chains, optimising grids, directing capital.

Energy security is now an AI conversation.

#ADIPEC2026 will be a key forum for addressing system‑level resilience and advancing the infrastructure needed to withstand future shocks.

#ADIPEC #ADNOC #EnergySystems #Resilience #Investment #ArtificialIntelligence

items.title: When you look for a job, you compete not only with thousands of other candidates, many of whom are u...
items.content:
When you look for a job, you compete not only with thousands of other candidates, many of whom are using automation to apply, but also with an AI-based system used by the employer to prescreen the applications.

If you want to maximize the chances your CV is seen by a human HR associate and minimize wasted time, it's important to make sure that your resume is built in a way that best corresponds to the job description. AI-based HR tools compare the skills, tools, technologies, and personal abilities shared by your resume and the job description and assign a higher score to those that have a larger intersection.

I've built an application that does exactly that: it compares your resume to the job description and proposes improvements that would make them look more alike.

Check it out: https://t.co/AUaysofZTg

By the way: The application is hosted on my new Llambada platform that allows anyone to build applications like that by simply talking to an AI agent. You can also customize any existing application by asking the AI agent to modify it the way you want.

If you build your own application and you think it might be useful to others, you can make it public and earn a revenue share when other people use it.

items.summary: Burkov announces an AI-powered resume optimization tool built on his 'Llambada' platform that compares resumes to job descriptions using embedding similarity. Also promotes Llambada as a platform for building AI apps via conversational agents with revenue sharing.
items.reasoning: Relevant product announcement from a known ML author. Good engagement (87 likes, 8K views). Touches on AI-powered job search and a new app-building platform.

Use Agents + OCR to Automate Compliance Reporting 📑🔐

This is an awesome project by @zubeensyed that...
items.content:
Use Agents + OCR to Automate Compliance Reporting 📑🔐

This is an awesome project by @zubeensyed that is able to ingest incident reports and match them against Article 33/34 notification requirements/risk thresholds.

items.title: I love to dunk on Apple, and Siri in particular, as much as the next guy, but I absolutely admire an...
items.content: I love to dunk on Apple, and Siri in particular, as much as the next guy, but I absolutely admire and respect the level of *consistent* quality and improvements that they’ve been able to pull off over the course of several decades. Absolutely unheard of in the tech world. They have been quietly but surely increasing their dominance in almost every sector in which they play - smartwatches, smartphones, tablets, laptops, desktops, etc. They don’t chase every last trend, and they probably leave a lot of good stuff on the table, but you get really impressive hardware and predictable ecosystem, year in year out. They might be too restrictive for developers, but for consumers the aer second to none.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz praises Apple's consistent quality and improvements over decades, admiring their dominance across hardware categories (watches, phones, tablets, laptops) despite not chasing every trend. Notes they may be restrictive for developers but unmatched for consumers.
items.reasoning: High engagement (292 likes, 40K views) — Tunguz's most engaged post in this batch. Thoughtful analysis of Apple's strategy, relevant to AI given Apple's AI integration approach. Contrasts their measured approach vs AI industry hype cycles.
items.summary_html: Tunguz praises Apple's consistent quality and improvements over decades, admiring their dominance across hardware categories (watches, phones, tablets, laptops) despite not chasing every trend. Notes they may be restrictive for developers but unmatched for consumers.
items.content_html: I love to dunk on Apple, and Siri in particular, as much as the next guy, but I absolutely admire and respect the level of *consistent* quality and improvements that they’ve been able to pull off over the course of several decades. Absolutely unheard of in the tech world. They have been quietly but surely increasing their dominance in almost every sector in which they play - smartwatches, smartphones, tablets, laptops, desktops, etc. They don’t chase every last trend, and they probably leave a lot of good stuff on the table, but you get really impressive hardware and predictable ecosystem, year in year out. They might be too restrictive for developers, but for consumers the aer second to none.

items.title:
Most teams think “more data = smarter AI.”

I make the opposite case: context beats volume.

When LL...
items.content:
Most teams think “more data = smarter AI.”

I make the opposite case: context beats volume.

When LLMs are grounded in your company’s own signals—not just the internet—they deliver accurate, explainable decisions at scale.

A thread on Context Engineering and why it changes enterprise intelligence (in partnership with @elastic). Let’s dig in…

#ElasticPartner

items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon's thread opener arguing that context beats volume for enterprise AI — LLMs grounded in company-specific signals deliver better results than those relying on general pretraining. Sponsored by Elastic.
items.content_html:
Most teams think “more data = smarter AI.”

I make the opposite case: context beats volume.

When LLMs are grounded in your company’s own signals—not just the internet—they deliver accurate, explainable decisions at scale.

A thread on Context Engineering and why it changes enterprise intelligence (in partnership with @elastic). Let’s dig in…

#ElasticPartner

items.title: @ImJustALaptop @Tesla_Optimus @1x_tech I'm not so sure about that. 1x is very expensive. Average hom...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer compares Tesla vs 1X Technologies for home robots, argues Tesla has manufacturing, distribution, and cost advantages plus robotaxi synergy
items.reasoning: Detailed competitive analysis between Tesla and 1X for home robotics market, mentions world models as key differentiator
items.summary_html: Scobleizer compares Tesla vs 1X Technologies for home robots, argues Tesla has manufacturing, distribution, and cost advantages plus robotaxi synergy
items.content_html:
@ImJustALaptop @Tesla_Optimus @1x_tech I'm not so sure about that. 1x is very expensive. Average homes don't have anything more than $2,000 in them.

Tesla has the ability to rent one to you for jobs due to Robotaxi in a way that 1x can't match. Also, 1x doesn't have several factories that will gear up, so can't cost reduce as quickly or get to scale as quickly and I don't believe it will be able to finish its world model in time, or build a brand or distribution that will get close to Tesla.

That said, it'll take years for Tesla to get there too, so will see if any of the newer humanoid-only companies can get there at any scale before Tesla figures out how to make millions per year and finish off its own world model.

items.title:
@himanshu__sriv You forget that Tesla has a huge factory in China. 

But, yeah, if competition does ...
items.content:
@himanshu__sriv You forget that Tesla has a huge factory in China. 

But, yeah, if competition does come it will be China. Which doesn't have the same advantages Tesla has in United States, which is still the richest country, so most likely to buy a lot of robots. 

And brand does matter. A lot. 

Tesla's robots already are on the road with about 10 million sold. No one else has matched that yet. And Tesla is making thousands more every day.

items.title:
The other big shift?

Owning and unifying the entire stack.

Enterprises don’t need another science ...
items.content:
The other big shift?

Owning and unifying the entire stack.

Enterprises don’t need another science experiment.
They need the easy button to operationalize AI.

That means:

→ Silicon
→ Optics
→ Systems
→ Orchestration layers

Working as one cohesive platform, not stitched together across 5 vendors and 10 roadmaps.

Fragmentation kills momentum.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.title:
Rakesh said something that stuck with me:

“AI infrastructure is about building a massive computer w...
items.content:
Rakesh said something that stuck with me:

“AI infrastructure is about building a massive computer with huge amounts of network bandwidth.”

Read that again.

AI isn’t just compute.

It’s:

→ Extreme east-west traffic
→ Massive bandwidth demands
→ Tight coupling between silicon, optics, systems, and orchestration

If your network is an afterthought, your AI strategy is too.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.title:
What is “Context Engineering”?

It’s the discipline of stitching your unstructured mess—logs, chats,...
items.content:
What is “Context Engineering”?

It’s the discipline of stitching your unstructured mess—logs, chats, docs, images—into something an AI can actually reason over.

→ Vector DBs + hybrid search + embeddings to retrieve by meaning, not keywords.

→ Decisions anchored in your data, not generic pretraining.

items.title: @seelaife When @stevewoz had the first color printer in Silicon Valley it cost $45,000 and even engi...
items.content:
@seelaife When @stevewoz had the first color printer in Silicon Valley it cost $45,000 and even engineers at Kodak couldn't see how that ever would be a consumer product (they kicked me out of their booth back in 1989 because I both knew more about their printer, and was more evangelistic about it than they were). 

Today a $70 printer is way way way better.

Silicon Valley knows how to cost reduce products. It's what we do. 

Stay alive a decade and you'll see Optimus won't be $20,000 (or more) for long.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer draws analogy between early expensive color printers ($45K) and Tesla Optimus pricing, arguing Silicon Valley excels at cost reduction over time
items.reasoning: Historical technology cost analogy applied to humanoid robots, with Woz anecdote. Makes case for rapid cost decline
items.summary_html: Scobleizer draws analogy between early expensive color printers ($45K) and Tesla Optimus pricing, arguing Silicon Valley excels at cost reduction over time
items.content_html:
@seelaife When @stevewoz had the first color printer in Silicon Valley it cost $45,000 and even engineers at Kodak couldn't see how that ever would be a consumer product (they kicked me out of their booth back in 1989 because I both knew more about their printer, and was more evangelistic about it than they were).

Today a $70 printer is way way way better.

Silicon Valley knows how to cost reduce products. It's what we do.

Stay alive a decade and you'll see Optimus won't be $20,000 (or more) for long.

Enter intelligent agents (the practical layer):

Ask in natural language. The agent pulls context across systems, then acts—send an alert, generate a report, flag an anomaly.

Result → faster cycles, clearer visibility, and automation you can trust across existing infrastructure.

items.title:
Here is what Depot CI gives you:

• Drop-in GitHub Actions compatibility. Run one command, and every...
items.content:
Here is what Depot CI gives you:

• Drop-in GitHub Actions compatibility. Run one command, and everything migrates without having to rewrite anything.

• Super fast CI. Depot's compute is optimized for performance.

• Custom runner images so you don't need to wait for the time it takes to download the stuff you don't need.

• Built-in debugging and monitoring.

• Everything is API-driven. You can trigger jobs with local file changes before you even commit.

This is not just a faster GitHub Actions. They actually built everything from scratch, including the orchestrator and compute layer.

Here is the announcement of Depot CI and the explanation of how it works:

https://t.co/iORf4un6gC

Thanks to the @depotdev team for partnering with me on this post.

items.summary: Santiago promotes Depot CI features in detail.

→ Accuracy scales because answers come from your ground truth.

→ You finally get explainability you can take to a board meeting.

items.title: Breaking News this week from @Commvault — read the details in the article links provided below the s...
items.content:
Breaking News this week from @Commvault — read the details in the article links provided below the summary.

Themes: #CyberResilience #IdentityResilience #ResOps #PowerOfCommunity

TL;DR —
#Commvault has announced a deepened integration with Microsoft Security to deliver an AI-powered, unified approach to cyber resilience, connecting real-time threat detection, investigation, and trusted recovery across security and backup operations. 

The enhanced Microsoft Sentinel connector now streams Commvault Cloud Threat Scan and Risk Analysis alerts—including malware detections, backup anomalies, and sensitive data exposures—directly into Sentinel for enriched SOC workflows, while the new Investigation Agent in Microsoft Security Copilot enables autonomous analysis of suspicious activity by correlating backup telemetry with broader Microsoft security signals.

This Commvault+Microsoft collaboration eliminates silos between IT recovery and security teams, accelerates mean time to clean recovery through coordinated, policy-driven workflows (with automation expanding in coming quarters), and empowers enterprises to achieve continuous resiliency in an evolving threat landscape—positioning Commvault as a strategic enabler of Agentic ResOps for faster, more confident data protection and business continuity. 

1) Commvault Expands Unified Resilience with Microsoft Sentinel Integration: https://t.co/GHPCQCdnpD

2) Commvault deepens Microsoft Security link for rapid recovery: https://t.co/A0SQ31amgd

#sponsored #RSAC #RSAC2026 #ResOpsRumble
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne provides a detailed summary of Commvault's deepened Microsoft Security integration for AI-powered cyber resilience, including Microsoft Sentinel connector and Investigation Agent in Security Copilot. Sponsored content.
items.reasoning: Detailed enterprise cybersecurity content but clearly sponsored. AI component is limited to Microsoft Copilot integration.

items.title: Yes, this is concerning. But hear me out: they are still an *extremely* small percentage of the popu...
items.content: Yes, this is concerning. But hear me out: they are still an *extremely* small percentage of the population, at least the extremely vile ones. All indicators of overt and latent racism have been on a steady decline over the decades. What allowing them to post their hatred online does is deprive them of the argument that their viewpoint is declining because it's somehow artificially suppressed and censored.

items.title: History showed that Einstein introduced the cosmological constant nearly 100 years ago to preserve a...
items.content:
History showed that Einstein introduced the cosmological constant nearly 100 years ago to preserve a static universe theory concept rather than favoring big bang with expanding cosmos with big bang theory. After evidence for cosmic expansion emerged, he abandoned that motivation altogether, which he called it as the “biggest blunder.” However that exact phrase is not directly documented in Einstein’s own writing. That wording is usually traced to George Gamow’s recollection.

items.title:
@ybouane @OnSiteEddie False:

- “Muslims guaranteed freedom of religion”: False — non-Muslims were d...
items.content:
@ybouane @OnSiteEddie False:

- “Muslims guaranteed freedom of religion”: False — non-Muslims were dhimmis (second-class status) with jizya tax, legal disadvantages, and restrictions; tolerance varied and was never equal.

- Locals “did not resist much” purely because of religious freedom: Overstated — conquest succeeded due to Visigothic collapse, military factors, and deals; there was resistance in places.

- Massacres mainly happened when Christians took back control: Misleading — both Muslim rule (Al-Andalus) and Christian Reconquista had periods of violence, persecution, and massacres.

- “Freedom of religion was guaranteed” in Muslim lands vs. Spanish oppression: Highly exaggerated binary — Muslim rule also had subordination and crackdowns (e.g., under Almohads); not modern-style freedom.
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: levelsio shares a detailed historical correction about Muslim rule in Iberia
items.reasoning: Completely off-topic from AI/ML
items.summary_html: levelsio shares a detailed historical correction about Muslim rule in Iberia
items.content_html:
@ybouane @OnSiteEddie False:

  • “Muslims guaranteed freedom of religion”: False — non-Muslims were dhimmis (second-class status) with jizya tax, legal disadvantages, and restrictions; tolerance varied and was never equal.
  • Locals “did not resist much” purely because of religious freedom: Overstated — conquest succeeded due to Visigothic collapse, military factors, and deals; there was resistance in places.
  • Massacres mainly happened when Christians took back control: Misleading — both Muslim rule (Al-Andalus) and Christian Reconquista had periods of violence, persecution, and massacres.
  • “Freedom of religion was guaranteed” in Muslim lands vs. Spanish oppression: Highly exaggerated binary — Muslim rule also had subordination and crackdowns (e.g., under Almohads); not modern-style freedom.

items.title:
I waited for 19 years to watch this movie with my kids.

Interstate 60 is one of the most mysterious...
items.content:
I waited for 19 years to watch this movie with my kids.

Interstate 60 is one of the most mysterious films I ever enjoyed. A $7M budget, written and directed by Bob Gale — the writer of the Back to the Future series — with Gary Oldman, Christopher Lloyd, Chris Cooper, Kurt Russell, and Michael J. Fox in the cast, and a box office of 8 **thousand** dollars!

One of the characters, Mr. Cody, played by Chris Cooper, was for me one of the most influential: "Say what you mean and mean what you say." This has become my motto since I watched the movie for the first time.

In addition to providing an answer to life that any teenager asks themselves at the age of 18-20, it's also a beautiful love story.

The fact that it hasn't got any serious viewership and remains known only to a handful of people despite being one of the best movies ever made is what I find mysterious about it. Maybe, like the movie's main feature: Interstate 60 that is only discoverable by those who look for it, this movie is also waiting for those who want to find it.

Now, if you follow me, you have a chance to find it too: https://t.co/97kx0aurK2

In addition to providing an answer to life that any teenager asks themselves at the age of 18-20, it's also a beautiful love story.

The fact that it hasn't got any serious viewership and remains known only to a handful of people despite being one of the best movies ever made is what I find mysterious about it. Maybe, like the movie's main feature: Interstate 60 that is only discoverable by those who look for it, this movie is also waiting for those who want to find it.

Now, if you follow me, you have a chance to find it too: https://t.co/97kx0aurK2

items.title:
@crypt0lake It's simply not

The entire society is built around Malay Muslims who get preferential t...
items.content:
@crypt0lake It's simply not

The entire society is built around Malay Muslims who get preferential treatment

"Malaysia has an official affirmative action policy called Bumiputera (“sons of the soil”), enshrined in the constitution and expanded after the 1969 racial riots. It covers Malay Muslims and some indigenous groups, but in practice mostly benefits Muslim Malays (who make up ~55% of the population).

Kuala Lumpur has hundreds of mosques and suraus (smaller prayer rooms), and the azan (call to prayer) is broadcast via loudspeakers 5 times a day"

How can you be so wrong? Are you Muslim?

items.content_html:
@jaderanderson @0x4C756973 I think the problem with Portuguese food is that Portugal has been SO poor for SO long (over 100 years) that we don't really have the original high quality Portuguese food that existed in let's say 1600-1800

The original Portuguese brioche croissant is probably great but nowadays it tastes dry, stale and shit, like most Portuguese food in padarias etc

It's just not fresh, not high quality ingredients and everything is cheap and dry
